data.title: CGGR: Capital Group's Growth ETF Is Built For The Long-Term
data.description:
Introduction

The Capital Group Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:CGGR) is an actively managed fund with a 0.39% expense ratio, $14.52 billion in assets under management at the time of writing (August 2025), 100 equity holdings, and a 16% annual portfolio turnover rate. It's prospectus benchmark is the SP500, though it does not attempt to perfectly track it but rather outperform it. These and several other statistics highlighted below make CGGR an attractive choice for large-cap growth investors, and in the article below, I will describe how CGGR works, how it's performed since its inception over three years ago, and how its current fundamentals help answer key questions about which type of investor CGGR is designed for and its unique strengths and weaknesses. I hope you enjoy the read.

CGGR Overview

Fund Facts and Performance Snapshot

The statistics listed earlier, along with others, are shown in the graphic below, taken from CGGR's website. Since long-term capital appreciation is the primary investment objective, CGGR's distributions are both infrequent (June and December) and small, with a 30-day SEC yield of only 0.30% as of June 30, 2025. Total returns have been solid, with the fund delivering a 27.29% gain from August 2024 to July 2025, and that one-year return ranks #14/73 among the large-cap growth ETFs I track. In addition, its 60.10% two-year total return ranks #12/60, and its 92.03% three-year gain ranks #6/49.

Principal Investment Strategies

According to its summary prospectus, CGGR has the following principal investment strategies and risks:

1. The fund invests primarily in common stocks that appear to offer superior opportunities for long-term growth of capital.

2. The fund may invest up to 25% of its assets outside the United States, exposing investors to foreign currency risk, and may become non-diversified by investing in relatively few issuers.

3. Investing in growth-oriented stocks may involve larger price swings and greater potential for loss than other types of investments.

4. Multiple portfolio managers contribute to the fund's selections, each focusing on a specific segment. Known as The Capital System, CGGR's managers are as follows:

  • Julian N. Abdey
  • Paul Benjamin
  • Mark L. Casey
  • Irfan M. Furniturewala
  • Anne-Marie Peterson
  • Andraz Razen
  • Alan J. Wilson

The Capital System Effect On Sector Allocations

The Capital System provides investors with diversification benefits not often seen in other funds. Diversifying by manager reduces the chances that one particular manager is off-base, and judging from the linked bios above, the current setup works well from a growth perspective. For example, 8/11 GICS sectors are covered by at least one portfolio manager, and tech coverage is extensive, with Benjamin, Casey, and Furniturewala specializing in software, hardware, and semiconductor stocks. As a result, it's no surprise to see that Technology comprised 27.2% of the fund as of June 30, 2025.

In contrast, CGGR has negligible exposure to Materials, Utilities, and Real Estate, three sectors not listed in any of the portfolio managers' bios. This may be viewed negatively as a bias for only "investing in what you know," but at the same time, these aren't high-growth sectors.

CGGR's "single issuer" risk is demonstrated in the top ten holdings list above, with Meta Platforms (META) accounting for 9.4% of the fund. Also included are Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), and Tesla (TSLA), and the top ten holdings accounted for 44.8% of the portfolio as of June 30, 2025. This concentration level seems high, but it's actually on the low end, as the average for the 77 large-cap growth ETFs I track is 51.25%.

CGGR Analysis

Periodic Returns vs. Large-Cap Growth ETF Peers

The following table expands on CGGR's performance history by including its one-month, three-month, six-month, one-year, two-year, three-year, and cumulative returns since March 2022 against 24 of the most popular large-cap growth ETFs by assets under management. The data is as of July 31, 2025, and demonstrates its success to date over the medium term. Notably, its 68.23% cumulative return from March 2022 to July 2025 ranks #12/46 and is close to the returns of better-known but more concentrated names like the Vanguard Mega Cap Growth ETF (MGK), the iShares Russell Top 200 Growth ETF (IWY), the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), and the Vanguard Russell 1000 Growth ETF (VONG).

CGGR's Potential Advantage Is Diversification

I mentioned earlier how CGGR's concentration in its top ten holdings, while high, is still on the low end. The reason is that it has far less exposure to "giant" cap stocks, defined as those falling within the top 40% by cumulative market cap, than its peers. To demonstrate, consider the size distribution table below, which shows CGGR having 22.25% less allocated to this group versus MGK, IWY, SCHG, and VONG. The offset is in the large-cap (next 30%) and mid-cap (next 20%) categories, and CGGR even has some exposure to the bottom 10% (small/micro-cap) of the U.S. stock market. This all wraps up nicely into CGGR's $1.09 trillion weighted average market cap figure, which, while still enormous, is $829 billion below average.

Next, let's circle back to CGGR's sector allocations. In the table below, I've updated these allocations as of August 6, 2025, and although Technology is still the largest sector at 29.29%, you'll notice this allocation is far less than the peer group. In exchange, CGGR allocates more to Communication Services (+7.72%), Industrials (+7.02%), and Health Care (+4.92%), and overall, this results in a relatively strong 6.00/10 sector diversification score. For those unfamiliar, this is an internal statistic I calculate that compares an ETF's sector allocations with a perfectly diversified (i.e., equal weight by sector) fund and a perfectly undiversified (i.e., single sector) fund.

In short, CGGR is better diversified than similar-performing passive ETFs based on its single issuer risk and sector exposures, so it's potentially a good way to take some risk off the table without sacrificing results.

CGGR Fundamental Analysis

Next, let's look closer at CGGR's fundamentals because there are some tradeoffs to consider. The table below highlights selected metrics for its top 25 holdings, which comprise 65.55% of the portfolio. Have a quick scan, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

CGGR: Still A Volatile ETF

The weighted average five-year beta of CGGR's current holdings is 1.40, indicating it has substantially more market risk than a broad market fund. The same is true for the other four large-cap growth peers, and as shown below, these ETFs lagged behind the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) by 8-12% in the last ten months of 2022. Therefore, investors should be prepared for big losses but may also experience large single-year gains, like in 2023 and 2024.

CGGR: Competitive Growth Rates With A Valuation Discount

CGGR's growth rates are competitive but not necessarily better than those of its peers. For example, its constituents have increased earnings per share by 18.91% over the last three years, and while this is excellent, it's about 1-2% less than the peer group. The same is true for its 21.40% one-year estimated earnings growth rate, but what makes CGGR potentially attractive is its discounted valuation. CGGR currently trades at 27.34x forward earnings in part due to its lesser tech sector exposure, and when dividing this ratio by the forward earnings growth rate, it's actually the most attractive of the five:

  • CGGR: 27.34x / 21.40% = 1.28x
  • MGK: 32.66x / 24.11% = 1.35x
  • IWY: 31.94x / 23.37% = 1.37x
  • SCHG: 30.45x / 22.89% = 1.33x
  • VONG: 31.68x / 23.45% = 1.35x

In addition, Seeking Alpha Factor Grades indicate CGGR is most attractive on both factors. While these grades are not available for ETFs, converting the grades to numbers for all components and weighting them accordingly nets the following:

The reason is that Seeking Alpha Factor Grades are sector adjusted, so in essence, CGGR's growth rates are competitive despite allocating more to lower-growth sectors like Health Care. To briefly illustrate, the growth score for the Health Care Select SPDR ETF (XLV) is only 3.76/10, but the score for the Health Care sector selections within CGGR is 6.51/10, demonstrating how CGGR's managers make their comparisons against other sector peers. This makes sense, too. Andraz Razen, the manager with a strong background in health care, likely isn't comparing Eli Lilly (LLY) with Nvidia (NVDA). Instead, Razen combines his expertise with the other six managers to create a well-diversified and fundamentally sound portfolio.

CGGR: Quality May Be Sacrificed

In pursuit of strong diversification, growth, and value features, CGGR managers appear to sacrifice some quality, which might explain why its 2022 results weren't better. Several statistics support this idea, including lower net margins and weaker return on assets and equity figures, as summarized in the table below.

CGGR's margins are several points below average, its weighted average asset turnover ratio (sales/assets) is 0.14 points lower, and debt as a percentage of free cash flow is slightly higher. Even after adjusting for sector composition differences with Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, CGGR's 8.99/10 profit score is 0.73 points below average, and that's simply what happens when you add more mid-cap stocks to the mix. Take Cloudflare, Inc. (NET), for example. While its growth prospects are high, it's currently one of 14/99 stocks (7.44% by weight) with negative net margins, hence its lower "B-" Grade. You can get better quality by sticking primarily with the mega-cap stocks, as only 4/68 stocks (1.67% by weight) in MGK have negative net margins.

Summary and Conclusion

This article was written primarily for educational purposes and, as such, did not include a rating. It discusses what type of returns can investors expect in bull and bear markets, so that investors can better understand if and how they may use it in their portfolio. My main objective was to answer questions like:

  • What type of investor is CGGR designed for?
  • How The Capital Group's multi-manager approach may benefit investors?
  • How does CGGR compare fundamentally against lower-cost passive ETFs?

My review indicates CGGR has a diversification advantage over its peers based on its lower concentration in its top ten holdings, its broad sector coverage, its overweighting of mid-caps at the expense of mega-caps, and the ability of its managers to invest internationally, with current exposure sitting at 8%. I believe this is linked to CGGR's multi-manager approach, where each manager is responsible for only a portion of the fund's holdings, and I also believe CGGR's seven managers have diverse enough backgrounds that suit a large-cap growth strategy well. For a 0.39% expense ratio, this feature alone might be worth the cost.

Although its track record is short, CGGR's poorer returns in 2022 versus the S&P 500 Index suggest it's a higher-risk product. My fundamental analysis revealed a high 1.40 five-year portfolio beta and lower quality as likely reasons, but its advantages include a discounted 27.34x forward P/E ratio and competitive growth rates, especially when considering its sector composition. This mix has served shareholders well, with CGGR's 92.03% three-year total return through July 2025 ranking #6/49 among the large-cap growth ETFs I track, and if you already own a low-cost passive fund, CGGR can serve as a nice complement. Its overlap with MGK, IWY, SCHG, and VONG is between 45% and 50%.

I hope I have answered the above questions, but if anything is unclear or you are interested in seeing how CGGR compares or complements other ETFs, please let me know in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the Fund for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about this ETF.

data.title: Twilio: Full Steam Ahead In Q2, Buy The Dip
data.description:
With the S&P 500 hovering near all-time highs amid a volatile Q2 earnings season, investors are rightly nervous about rich valuations amid a possibly shaky economy. In my view, now is the time to invest with extreme caution: But the way to do that, in my view, is by rotating away from the large-cap momentum plays that have dominated the S&P 500's gains this year to less-crowded small/mid-cap plays, where fundamental quality and valuation are far more likely to be disconnected.

Twilio Inc. (NYSE:TWLO) is a prominent example of this. The communications platform-as-a-service company, which develops software that helps businesses design and execute communications to their customers, fell ~10% after reporting a very strong Q2 that hit all the metrics that investors needed to see improving, including organic growth and net retention rates. The post-earnings reaction has virtually wiped out all of Twilio's YTD gains.

I last wrote an article on Twilio in May, when the stock was beginning a rebound as it started to print improving organic growth results. I'll cut to the chase: I see no red flags at all in Twilio's latest quarter (besides the fact that the company didn't raise its margin expectations alongside strengthening organic growth), and I'm more than happy to take the opportunity to add to my Twilio position on the dip. In my view, Twilio's slide has more to do with investors seeking safety in crowded large-cap trades and avoiding the perception of riskier small/mid-cap stocks. But over the medium to long-term timeframe, I believe TWLO is overdue for an upward re-rating. I'm reiterating my "Buy" position here.

It's important to recognize, off the get-go, that Twilio's post-earnings fall comes against the context of raised guidance that makes its valuation look even more appealing. At current post-earnings share prices near $108, Twilio trades at a market cap of $16.54 billion. After we net off the $2.54 billion of cash (much of which is earmarked for buybacks) and $991.4 million of debt on Twilio's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $14.99 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, Twilio has boosted its full-year revenue outlook to 10%-11% y/y growth (above consensus' 8.9% expectations, and representing $4.93 billion in revenue at the midpoint), on the back of 9%-10% organic growth, which is a 150bps improvement from the company's prior expectations.

It also slightly nudged up its FCF range to $875-$900 million (18% FCF margins), $25 million higher than its prior range, while it has maintained its pro forma operating margin expectations (which is the only potential downside, as this implies a slightly weaker pro forma operating margin against raised revenue expectations - but given the FCF boost, I'm hardly concerned here).

For next year, FY26, Wall Street is currently expecting Twilio to generate $5.21 billion in revenue (+6% y/y). If we assume Twilio can hit a 20% FCF margin next year (in line with the company's current ~2 point y/y expansion in operating margins), FCF on that revenue profile would be $1.04 billion (+17% y/y). This positions Twilio's valuation multiples at:

  • 3.0x EV/FY25 revenue and 2.9x EV/FY26 revenue.
  • 16.9x EV/FY25 FCF and 14.4x EV/FY26 FCF.

To me, the opportunity to buy a recurring-revenue software company with firming organic growth and net retention rates at just a low/mid-teens forward FCF multiple is quite appealing, especially in today's very expensive stock market.

This is an excellent "buy the dip" opportunity, in my view. Stay long here and use the opportunity to add to your Twilio position.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Twilio's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Twilio's revenue grew 13% y/y to $1.23 billion, well ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $1.19 billion (+10% y/y) by a three-point margin.

We note that Twilio has improved its organic revenue growth trends for four straight quarters. Recall that Twilio has been in the "penalty box" for several years, in particular due to lagging performance in its Segment division, which is a customer data platform that struggled amid sharp competition as well as sales execution issues.

Segment is still problematic: In Q2, its revenue of $75 million was flat y/y - but it's now a much smaller portion (just 6%) of Twilio's overall revenue. The company has focused back on its roots as a messaging platform, which has helped the company accelerate its organic growth by one point relative to 12% growth in Q1. Relative to last year, when Twilio grew at a 7% pace organically in Q2'24, Twilio's growth rates have roughly doubled.

Core to this improvement is a recovery in the company's dollar-based net expansion rates. Recall that Twilio is entirely a usage-based platform: customers pay Twilio based on the number of messages they send, or the minutes of voice communications are activate on its platform. This makes Twilio's ability to "land and expand" and grow from within its customer base essential.

As shown in the chart below, net expansion rates have trended upward to 108% in the most recent quarter, versus a prior-year period that showed barely any net expansion. The communications segment showed an even healthier 109% net expansion, while the overall company was still weighed down by churn issues in the Segment division, which had a 95% net expansion rate (indicating 5% of the customer base ended up churning out).

We note that Twilio's improving net retention rates are impressive in a tough economy, where many companies have put a tighter lid on budgets. I would also consider Twilio's core use case (messaging customers) as highly discretionary, something that businesses can cut immediately without friction to save money - and hence, Twilio's improving performance as we move through a tough 2025 is quite reassuring.

And despite the market's concerns about Twilio's failure to raise its operating margin guidance for the year, in Q2 standalone, the company posted an 18% pro forma margin, which improved 2 points y/y. This is despite gross margin pressure (-260bps y/y to 50.7% on a pro forma basis), which is driven by the company's revenue mix shift toward the lower-margin communications segment (which has a ~49% margin) and away from Segment (which, as a pure software platform, has a mid-70s margin).

We note that Twilio's guidance, calling for $850-875 million in operating income, implies a 17.5% margin at the midpoint, versus 18.1% in the first half of FY25 - indicating potential upside ahead.

Risks and key takeaways

Of course, we should be cognizant of the risks that Twilio faces. The company has historically suffered from choppy execution in the past. As mentioned, corporate spending on customer messaging is highly discretionary, and companies pressured to cut IT costs could suddenly choose to reduce spending on Twilio, which could hurt its recent trend of improving organic growth and net retention rates (that said, we haven't seen this happen yet).

To me, risks for TWLO are already more than priced into the stock, which is very cheap at ~3x revenue and a mid-teens multiple of FCF. This post-earnings dip is despite an impressive beat-and-raise: take advantage of Twilio's dislocation between fundamentals and value to buy.

data.title: ePlus: Strong Quarter, Stronger Case For A Re-Rating
data.description:
ePlus inc. (NASDAQ:PLUS) just dropped their Q1 FY26 earnings, and the results were nothing short of impressive. The company not only beat on both the top and bottom lines, but also unveiled strategic moves (which I’ll talk about down the line in this article) that could reshape how investors view the stock going forward.

Q1 FY26 was a blowout quarter across the board as the financial figures show. ePlus recorded a 19% YoY jump in the top line; net sales was $637.3 million, beating the consensus estimates of $534 million by $120.6 million.

ePlus also saw a 12% YoY increase in the bottom line. Net earnings from continuing operations was $27.1 million. Non-GAAP EPS was at $1.26, beating consensus expected EPS by $0.16. Adjusted EBITDA also increased by 19.6% YoY to $46.7 million. ePlus also saw a balance sheet boost from the divesting of one its business units (more details later in this article), which boosted cash and equivalents to $480.2 million, up by $90.8 million from Q4 FY25.

For readers who may not be familiar with ePlus, and wonder what kind of company delivers these impressive numbers, ePlus is a technology solutions provider that has been in the enterprise IT and infrastructure business for over three decades. ePlus offers a comprehensive portfolio of enterprise tech solutions, which include AI, security, cloud and data center, networking and collaboration, as well as managed, consultative, and professional services. ePlus works with organizations across various verticals to help them optimize their IT environments. To put it short, ePlus can be seen as a tech consulting and hybrid VAR (value-added reseller) company.

Until Q1 FY26, ePlus operated its business through two main segments: the technology segment, where it offers IT products and a wide range of services, including AI, cybersecurity, cloud and data center, and managed and professional services; and the financing segment, where it offers direct financing, sales-type, and operating leases for IT equipment. The financing segment business has been divested to PEAC business solutions and was the main reason for the boost in cash and equivalents to $480.2 million (which I mentioned earlier). This divestiture was done to enable ePlus core higher-margin tech solutions business, and to provide additional capital for growth and acquisitions, and simplify its overall business model. The company has said it still offers financial services to its customers and vendors via a partnership with PEAC, but without the heavy expenses that came with running its own financing business.

An important detail also worth mentioning for context is that ePlus is a DGX SuperPOD Specialization Partner of NVIDIA. This is a highly specialized skill and capability area within the NVIDIA Partner Network. And the designation means that ePlus is part of a select group of partners who are qualified to design, deploy, and optimize NVIDIA's flagship AI platform, the DGX SuperPOD.

Why There's an Overlooked Opportunity in ePlus

Many investors will agree that the U.S. equity market is currently stretched and arguably overbought, with the S&P 500 (SPX) and even niche indexes hovering near or at all-time highs. There's increasing talk of a correction after the years-long tech rally, driven predominantly by mega-cap AI names like Nvidia (NVDA). While those fears are valid, there is always an overlooked opportunity in the markets, particularly among profitable but underappreciated mid-cap names.

In the past year, tech consulting and hybrid VAR firms like ePlus haven't had the same explosive run as pure-play AI stocks, and this is largely because enterprise discretionary IT budgets have been soft, especially in hardware and network infrastructure. With rates holding steady, higher financing costs are no longer accelerating, but CIOs have remained cautious in committing to large-scale IT refresh cycles. A macro view survey conducted by ETR in Q2 this year showed IT budget growth expectations plunging 190 basis points from the start of the year to Q2 (from 5.3 percent in January to 3.4 percent in April). ETR called it the “biggest drop they’ve ever recorded outside a crisis.” Still, I think we are approaching a bottoming in the weak enterprise IT spending sentiment and procurement deferrals, and here’s why.

Service revenue is proving resilient for tech consulting and hybrid VAR firms, implying that customers may delay hardware upgrades, but demand for cybersecurity, cloud migration, and AI infrastructure consulting is still expanding. Companies like ePlus, which derive a growing share of margin from services and recurring revenue, are better insulated from hardware demand shocks. And as the Q1 FY26 results show, the main driver from the top line was the acceleration of services revenue which increased 48.8% and was driven by the acquisition of Bailiwick Services LLC - an acquisition which has proved accretive.

The top narratives and drivers for this sustained momentum in service revenue have been enterprise investments in AI and cybersecurity initiatives. The shift toward AI requires integration expertise, not just chips, and firms like ePlus that partner with Nvidia are well positioned to be behind-the-scenes beneficiaries of enterprise AI rollouts and demand.

Tech Consulting Lags the Broader Tech Market

Most tech consulting and hybrid VAR stocks have been beat down in the past year, compared to the broader U.S. equity market.

PLUS and closest peers, CDW Corp. (CDW) and Insight Enterprises (NSIT), have trailed behind the S&P 500, and even further behind the more technology-focused S&P 500 Information Technology Sector Index (SP500-45TR).

Even an IT consulting and services firm like Accenture (ACN) with a broader global client footprint and service portfolio hasn't been rewarded proportionately in its stock price compared to the broader market. Though I wouldn't consider ACN a very direct peer to PLUS because its revenue mix is much more consulting-heavy, in this context the muted stock performance of ACN shows how the enterprise IT services sector lags broader market momentum, and shows how PLUS’s underperforming stock price despite stellar performance is not an isolated case.

But for patient investors looking beyond short-term market sentiment, the mispricing among these stocks could be a setup worth watching. And in ePlus’ case in particular, the stellar financials strengthen the long-term investment thesis.

In the Q1 FY26 results released yesterday, ePlus announced a share buy back authorization of up to 1.5 million shares, which is around 5.7% of the current ~26.5 million share count. At a current $63 share price, the program represents ~$95 million, or about 5.7% of the company’s $1.68 billion market cap.

To top that move, ePlus declared its first-ever quarterly cash dividend of $0.25 per share, which annualizes to $1.00 per share and equates to a 1.58% dividend yield at $63 current stock price. The move is a major signal of confidence in free cash flow generation, and will potentially attract income-focused investors.

And there is a lot ePlus could do with the significant cash flow which increased by 23.3% over the last quarter, and stood at $295 million [TTM] as of Q1 FY26 end. From accelerating share buy backs to accretive acquisitions to capture or expand market share in more margin-rich verticals, the path is clear for ePlus with the type of cash flow flexibility it now possesses. And each of these capital allocation moves are all accretive levers for EPS and for shareholder value creation.

The Case for a Re-Rating

While PLUS and its closest peers have been beat down and all trade at lower valuation multiples compared to some other tech sectors, PLUS’ healthy revenue growth rate and superior free cash flow yield are two most important metrics in my comp framework to gauge PLUS’ undervaluation relative to its close peers CDW and NSIT. PLUS is growing its top line and generates significantly more free cash flow relative to its market value.

The icing on the cake is that management is confident in sustaining this trend. Guidance for fiscal 2026 has been raised. Management is now expecting net sales and gross profit to grow in the upper single digits and adjusted EBITDA to increase in the mid-teens. This was raised from a more conservative guidance of low single-digit net sales growth and mid-single-digit EBITDA increase as of Q4 FY25 end. A lot went right in Q1 and has strengthened the rest of FY26 outlook.

In short, ePlus is a profitable company that is well-managed and is successfully transforming its business for a services-led, consistent high-margin future. The stock's current discount (which is partly sector sentiment driven), combined with its strong growth and a new shareholder-friendly capital return strategy, makes the case for a meaningful re-rating upward hard to ignore, in my view.

Risks

While Q1 results and revised guidance are attractive, several risks could weigh on execution or valuation. At the macro level, enterprise IT spending remains sensitive to economic conditions, and a deeper-than-expected slowdown in clients’ IT budgets could ignite procurement deferrals among clients. Also, prolonged high interest rates or a reversal in credit conditions could also impact clients’ financing appetite, even with the PEAC partnership in place.

Takeaways

ePlus just delivered the kind of quarter that forces the market to take notice. The market is still pricing PLUS like a slow-growth, hardware-dependent VAR, but the numbers and strategic moves tell a very different story. For investors willing to look past near-term sector sentiment and macro noise, the setup here is compelling: strong execution, superior growth vs. peers, and multiple levers for value creation. In my view, this is the kind of asymmetry you want as an investor - limited downside from current levels (PLUS is trading ~40% down from its 52 weeks high), and meaningful upside if the re-rating thesis plays out.

data.title: Webco Industries: Free Cash Flow Virtually Dried Up, New Stock Buybacks Tough
data.description:
A Hold Rating For Webco Industries, Inc.

This analysis supports a Hold rating for the U.S.-listed shares of Webco Industries, Inc. (OTCPK:WEBC).

Let's first describe Webco using a company statement. The earnings release for their third quarter of fiscal year 2025, which ended April 30, 2025, states:

We provide high-quality carbon steel, stainless steel and other metal specialty tubing products designed to industry and customer specifications. We have five tube production facilities in Oklahoma and Pennsylvania and eight value-added facilities in Oklahoma, Illinois, Michigan, Pennsylvania and Texas, serving customers globally.

Our Vision For Webco: Create Value For Shareholders but Profit Under Pressure From Challenging Business Conditions

In its latest quarterly results, the stock showed signs of recovery from the sharp decline in U.S. manufacturing activity, on which its profitability depends. Webco's attractiveness lies in the value it generates through the repurchase of its own common shares on the stock market.

What more could investors want in their attempts to profit from Webco's share buyback programs than the fact that the market value of Webco shares tends to increase over time? For this scenario of a rising stock price to occur, we assume that the contribution of growing corporate profits is the most important favorable factor, as generally recognized by U.S. equity analysts.

Rising profits create demand for Webco shares in the market, as the message is clear to the participants: Cash flow is being generated to finance growth investments, but not only that. The cash flow could also soon enable share buybacks, i.e., before the company's expected growth plans deliver the desired results. The repurchase of own ordinary shares is warmly welcomed by the market because, together with the dividend distribution (which Webco does not finance), it is the clearest evidence of the value a company can create and return to its shareholders. The share price is therefore well-positioned to benefit from the uptrend.

How Webco's Profit Develops Under Challenging Business Conditions

As previously mentioned, there are signs of improvement in Webco's profitability. Profitability, when it smiles, supports the buyback program, but in our opinion, this improvement is still a long way off, as the company has not dared to revive its “Webco share buyback program,” which, on the contrary, appears to be dragging on until expiration.

Before analyzing Webco's margins, the company's Q3 FY 2025 earnings report clearly shows that the success of the company's plans to distribute value to its various stockholders depends on profitability. The company highlights the reduction in stakeholder compensation expenses that had to be implemented due to lower-than-normal profitability. Share buybacks are different from programs that reward employees with value, but as stockholders, investors in Webco, as in any other company, are ultimately subject to the same principle.

The company states:

[…] SG&A expenses in fiscal year 2025 reflect decreases in costs related to lower profitability, such as company-wide incentive compensation and variable pay programs, offset by inflation we have experienced in wages and other expenses.

Profit Margins In The Q3 and 9-Month of Fiscal Year 2025 and Their Trend Interpretations

The margins also suggest that profitability may be trending upwards again, based on the third quarter of fiscal 2025 (or the 3-month period ending April 2025) compared to the third quarter of fiscal 2024 (or the 3-month period ending April 2025) although on a nine-month basis, things are moving too slowly to allow for the adjustment one might optimistically hope for. This is what is immediately obvious to the reader, but the discerning eye of sophisticated investors will notice two things that would not yet allow for a return to a completely optimistic mood about Webco's value creation program.

Backed by the following two aspects, which many are unlikely to notice, sophisticated investors will probably argue that

  • since the April 3-month period tends to be better than its previous 3-month period (see screenshot below), the improvement in Q3 FY 2025 compared to Q3 FY 2024 could be the result of pure seasonality, carrying the risk of making the mistake of believing that Webco is truly recovering from the more than two years that in the company’s eyes reflected a recession in the domestic manufacturing sector.

  • Q3 FY 2025 is better than Q3 FY 2024 on two out of three profitability margins (see the screenshot), but is still significantly lower than the corresponding 3-month periods in years further back in time. Thus, the third quarter of fiscal 2025 loses ground across the board compared to the third quarter of fiscal 2023. This signal should not be underestimated. It could indicate that the company is still struggling to restore profitability and resume a long-term upward trend. Contrary to what one might think after the third quarter of fiscal 2025 earnings figures.

Therefore, we must proceed with caution and avoid reckless enthusiasm, which usually leads to decisions dictated by impulsiveness rather than rationality.

New Buyback Programs Unlikely On Virtually Dried-Up Free Cash Flow

Consistent with the idea of a company still struggling to regain the profitability levels during the boom phase of the business cycle, cash flow deteriorated significantly between fiscal years 2024 and 2025.

Investments in the construction and expansion of Webco's stainless steel facilities in fiscal year 2025 actually consumed fewer resources than in fiscal year 2024 for the construction of the Tech Center and the company's headquarters. The core of the issue, however, is that cash flow from operations has declined remarkably. This represents a weak performance objectively, and perhaps because of this, the company may currently feel not able to undertake further commitments and authorize the distribution of more value to the market through buyback programs.

Maybe this situation will persist until a genuine recovery increases profitability and cash flow. Free cash flow, which we (not the company) have calculated as cash flow from operations minus capital expenditures, and which is used to compensate shareholders, has virtually dried up, as the screenshot illustrates.

In fact, Webco appears to be reluctant to make any predictions about possible future buyback programs. An overview of the shares already purchased and the number remaining, a small volume in fact, until the expiration of the existing repurchase authorization, is the only thing we have from Webco gotten so far here, apart from the standard statement that:

The repurchase plan may be extended, suspended or discontinued at any time, without notice, at the Board's discretion. (according to Webco Industries, Inc.’s third-quarter fiscal year 2025 results).

The repurchase program is authorized until July 31, 2026. Based on the market value of Webco's U.S.-listed common shares at the time of this writing and with approximately $4.9 million of remaining purchase authorization under the current share repurchase program as of April 30, 2025, Webco Industries, Inc. will repurchase approximately 26,344 shares of common stock over the next 12 months. A repurchase prospect that, in terms of potential share price upside, can hardly be considered as attractive as the 18,000-share repurchase plan in just three months of the third quarter of fiscal year 2025 (ended April 30, 2025) or the 166,000-share repurchase plan in the first nine months of fiscal year 2025.

This does not inspire optimism and therefore cannot encourage the purchase of Webco shares at this time.

The future outlook also currently does not indicate that the company will announce a renewal of its shareholder value distribution program in the near future.

Additional Headwinds Loom For New Stock Buyback Authorization

A genuine recovery in the U.S. manufacturing industry is not an option, as the resulting increase in corporate profits could convince markets sufficiently that the company will launch a new share buyback program. This is the hope, but the company itself indicates that the environment for the manufacturing industry remains difficult. While the situation appears to be slowly recovering from an earlier period of contraction in manufacturing activity, it is the uncertainty surrounding President Trump's tariffs, intended to counter unfair dumping of foreign products in Webco's markets, that is now dampening sentiment in the manufacturing sector.

The share price will benefit from the potentially positive effects of the resumption of the share buyback program once the manufacturing industry is no longer facing headwinds. But until then, the tariffs will ensure that uncertainty continues among manufacturing companies, potentially weighing on the share price of Webco.

So, we currently consider a Hold rating for Webco stock to be appropriate as the company is expected to create value for its investors once the challenges in the manufacturing industry are overcome. But the risk of a share price being subject to downward pressure reflecting the lack of bite in a buyback plan pending a more convincing return to profitability continues as long as negative uncertainties affect the near-term outlook for the business environment. Dana S. Weber, CEO and Chairman of the Board of Directors, describes a market environment that continues to offer Webco opportunities but also challenges. A Hold rating currently best reflects this corporate vision.

In commenting on the third quarter of fiscal year 2025 financial results, Dana S. Weber stated:

[…] The dynamic and evolving tariff environment, which affect each of our customers and vendors very differently, presents numerous opportunities and challenges. […]

The Balance Sheet Can Navigate Challenging Short-Term Future

It will take some time for profitability to return to pre-crisis levels. Therefore, a solid financial position is crucial for Webco to master the near future, which will still be fraught with major challenges. This will provide a competitive advantage over foreign companies doomed to fail as the era of unfair dumping comes to an end.

In commenting on the third quarter of fiscal year 2025 financial results, Dana S. Weber stated:

Our total cash, short-term investments and available credit on our revolver were $75.7 million at April 30, 2025, which we believe to be a competitive advantage."

The company further specifies its financial situation and writes as follows:

As of April 30, 2025, we had $15.0 million in cash and short-term investments, in addition to $60.7 million of available borrowing under our $220 million senior revolving credit facility. Availability on the revolver, which had $80.7 million drawn at April 30, 2025, is subject to advance rates on eligible accounts receivable and inventories. Our term loan and revolver mature in September 2027. Accounting rules require asset-based debt agreements like our revolver to be classified as a current liability, despite its fiscal year 2028 maturity.

In the third quarter of fiscal year 2025, Webco had receivables of $79.7 million and net inventories of $174 million. Let's start with net inventories, as they are roughly the same as the corresponding quarter of fiscal year 2024. As the 2024 fiscal year was marked by the negative effects of the production slowdown, the largely unchanged inventories since then do not indicate that the company expects an improvement in demand for its products. Higher inventories would instead indicate an improvement, but this is still not the scenario at the moment.

With regard to the accounts receivable, these piled up 13.69% year-on-year in the third quarter of fiscal year 2025. Given that total revenues went up at substantially smaller growth rate than receivables, precisely they were up 1.40% year-on-year in the third quarter of fiscal year 2025, we believe this visible discrepancy is consistent with the ongoing challenging business environment in which customers are struggling to pay their invoices, resulting in Webco's sales with longer credit terms on average.

Let's analyze some solvency ratios to see how well Webco compares to the sector in terms of its ability to meet the challenges of the near future.

First, Webco has an Altman Z-score of 2.75 (see the “Risk” section of this Seeking Alpha page), which is very close to the green zones and means there is no risk of insolvency in the coming years. This metric measures the probability of a company becoming insolvent: A score below 1.8 signals crisis areas with a high probability of default. A score between 1.8 and 2.9 indicates gray areas with a medium to low probability of insolvency. A score of 3.0 or higher signals green or safe zones and no risk of insolvency.

Second, Webco's 12-month Interest Coverage Ratio is calculated as follows: 12-month operating income through April 2025 of $11.9 million divided by 12-month interest expense through April 2025 of $4.9 million. This ratio is 2.43, which is close to the minimum requirement of 1.5 to 2.0 that the value investing community requires a business to have on its skillset to be considered economically viable and able to meet all its debt obligations.

Third, the following screenshot of Webco’s long-term solvency suggests that the company has low financial risk because it relies on equity and internally generated funds rather than debt. The company is not highly leveraged and does not have large financial obligations such as principal and interest, so the risk of insolvency is under control. This also puts the company in a strong position as it may need debt for future growth. Lenders are expected to meet the company’s needs, while the improved business environment will in the future support earnings growth that lenders will surely appreciate.

Stock Price: In principle, Hold, But Continue To Target Dips

At the time of writing, Webco Industries, Inc.'s U.S.-listed stock, trading under the ticker symbol “WEBC”, was trading at $186 per share, giving it a market capitalization of $130.82 million.

We continue to see uncertainties regarding Webco's business, and in this environment it is not easy for the company to find the monetary resources to fund its programs of value creation for the market and the economy. In comparison, the market value of Webco's publicly traded shares in the US trades currently at a high level, based on recent trends and some metrics, which we will describe as follows: At the time of writing, the shares were trading well above the midpoint of $177/share in the 52-week range of $150/share to $204/share. Also, the shares were trading above the Ribbon MA.

Let's look at the 14-RSI trend over the past 12 months. We see the possibility that the shares could decline due to volatility reaching an oversold level of an RSI of 30, and the environment is highly unstable now that Webco’s RSI is 52.36. An oversold level implies a share price of around $160/share, as it was around April 23-24, 2025.

Building a long-term position in Webco to benefit from shareholder value programs such as own common stock buybacks should be done by taking advantage of sharp price declines (or “dips”). This strategy positions the holding well in advance of buyback programs and allows for mitigating the impact of the stock's illiquidity on the US stock market. The “Trading Data” section of the Seeking Alpha website lists the average volume over a three-month period at 13. The downside is that an investor who needs to sell shares due to unexpected circumstances but did not buy them on the dips could find himself in a difficult position due to the extremely limited scope for action. He would then have to lower his request significantly below the price paid at the time of purchase to have a chance of executing the order. This could result in unpleasant trading losses.

Possible Trigger For Another Dip: The Analysis

Webco's share price decline was likely triggered by speculation that the Trump administration's 25 per cent tariffs on U.S. steel and aluminum were the final straw. The tariffs prompted already struggling steel giants like POSCO Holdings Inc. (PKX), ArcelorMittal S.A. (MT), and Tata Steel Limited (OTC:TATLY) to crack down on China and Chinese imports. This hurt the U.S. steelmakers' global customers, including Webco Industries. With China most likely still the world's largest steel market, the headwinds from the speculation drove the share price to plummet.

These headwinds persist, as current economic conditions favor speculation and fuel the irrational behavior of many financial market participants. First, given the continued negative data from Germany, the Eurozone's leading economy and home to Europe's largest manufacturing sector, steel producers in Europe cannot yet claim that the bad times are behind them. Factory orders in Germany declined for the second consecutive month in June 2025 due to weak domestic and international demand, and across all sectors.

In U.S.: To anticipate global tariff increases, the front-loaded demand stimulates trade activity and pushes steel product prices sharply higher in the U.S. market as well. This could lead to a new trend among U.S. customers of U.S. steel producers, including Webco, necessitating longer payment terms for their orders. This means that the slower conversion of receivables to cash must be offset by an increase in the order backlog, which the current situation in the U.S. manufacturing industry can’t yet support. And with stock market participants aware of the challenge, tension remains high, and in such situation it won't take much actually for somewhat deteriorating sentiment to trigger a plunge in Webco's stock.

Conclusion

Webco Industries, Inc., headquartered in Sand Springs, Oklahoma, has been manufacturing and distributing carbon steel, stainless steel, and other tubing products for the power generation, oil and gas, process, automotive, and industrial pipeline industries since 1969. Most of these industries are currently struggling to recover from the troughs of their business cycles, as the U.S. manufacturing sector as a whole is trying to emerge from more than two years of economic recession.

Many of Webco's other international customers are experiencing the same situation, compounded by the short-term negative effects of the global trade war. The tariffs imposed by U.S. President Trump are intended to prevent unfair dumping of products by foreign manufacturers into U.S. markets. The long-term goal of these measures is to create a level playing field for U.S. steel companies in the domestic market, and Webco believes it is seeing the first positive signs of this strategy for the U.S. markets, not just its drawbacks. However, we believe Webco’s profitability remains under pressure, and it is perhaps no coincidence that the company has not provided any indication of an extension of its authorization to create shareholder value for the markets through its own common stock buyback programs. The current program expires in twelve months.

The share price could decline before the global economic situation improves and Webco's stock buyback programs resume. Therefore, we believe the best way to view the stock at this stage is to hold it, as the situation remains bleak and highly volatile.
